claim_text: Heavier social media use is associated with more depressive symptoms among adolescents.
snippet: report more depressive symptoms in large cross-sectional surveys
label: supports
rationale: source reports the association directly

claim_text: Social media use causes depression in adolescents.
snippet: the study does not establish that social media use causes depression
label: unsupported
rationale: causal overclaim: source is correlational

claim_text: Social media has a large effect on adolescent depression.
snippet: Effects were small in absolute terms
label: contradicts
rationale: source reports small effects; claim says large

claim_text: Associations were stronger for girls than for boys.
snippet: stronger among girls than among boys
label: supports
rationale: directly stated

claim_text: Digital technology use is negatively associated with adolescent well-being.
snippet: the association between digital technology use and adolescent well-being was negative but very small
label: supports
rationale: snippet directly states a negative association; claim asserts direction only, not magnitude (Codex final review)

claim_text: Social media use explains most of the variance in adolescent well-being.
snippet: Social media use explained well under one percent of the variation in well-being
label: contradicts
rationale: source: <1%; claim: most

claim_text: Digital technology use causes lower adolescent well-being.
snippet: The authors caution against strong causal claims
label: unsupported
rationale: authors explicitly caution against causal claims

claim_text: Higher social media use is associated with greater perceived social isolation in young adults.
snippet: higher social media use was associated with greater perceived social isolation
label: supports
rationale: directly stated

claim_text: The use-isolation association was dose-dependent.
snippet: The relationship was dose-dependent across quartiles of use
label: supports
rationale: directly stated

claim_text: Social media use leads to social isolation.
snippet: cannot determine the direction of causation
label: unsupported
rationale: cross-sectional; causal direction not established

claim_text: Isolation effects are identical across all age groups.
snippet: national sample of young adults
label: unsupported
rationale: young adults only; no age-group comparison (out of scope)

claim_text: More social media time predicted later internalizing problems in a longitudinal cohort.
snippet: more time on social media predicted later internalizing problems
label: supports
rationale: directly stated, longitudinal

claim_text: Using social media more than three hours per day was linked to elevated internalizing symptom risk.
snippet: more than three hours per day had elevated risk of internalizing symptoms
label: supports
rationale: directly stated

claim_text: Social media use causes internalizing problems.
snippet: more time on social media predicted later internalizing problems
label: weakly_supports
rationale: snippet shows longitudinal temporal prediction (weak causal evidence); full causation not established (Codex final review: snippet-only)

claim_text: There is strong, consistent evidence that social media harms adolescent mental health.
snippet: the evidence linking adolescent social media use to mental health harm is weak and inconsistent
label: contradicts
rationale: source: weak & inconsistent; claim: strong & consistent

claim_text: Most reported associations between social media use and adolescent mental health are small.
snippet: Most reported associations are small
label: supports
rationale: directly stated

claim_text: Social media effects depend on the adolescent's personality.
snippet: of unclear practical significance
label: unclear
rationale: snippet does not address personality moderation; insufficient to judge

claim_text: Social media use reduces adolescents' academic performance.
snippet: public concern has outpaced the actual evidence
label: unsupported
rationale: source does not address academic performance (off-topic)

claim_text: Higher social media use was associated with depression, anxiety, and distress in adolescents.
snippet: associated with each of the three outcomes across the included studies
label: supports
rationale: directly stated across outcomes

claim_text: A systematic review examined depression, anxiety, and distress in adolescents in relation to social media.
snippet: This systematic review examined depression, anxiety, and psychological distress in adolescents
label: supports
rationale: descriptive claim, directly stated

claim_text: Social media use robustly causes adolescent depression.
snippet: substantial methodological heterogeneity and heavy reliance on self-report measures
label: unsupported
rationale: review of associations with heavy caveats; no causal claim

claim_text: Social media is the leading cause of adolescent anxiety.
snippet: associated with each of the three outcomes across the included studies
label: unsupported
rationale: causal + ranking overclaim ('leading cause'); snippet shows association only (Codex final review)

claim_text: Social media use markedly increases perceived social isolation in young adults.
snippet: higher social media use was associated with greater perceived social isolation
label: weakly_supports
rationale: snippet supports an association only; 'markedly increases' overstates strength and implies causation

claim_text: The link between social media use and depressive symptoms was stronger for adolescent girls than boys.
snippet: were stronger among girls than among boys
label: supports
rationale: Source states the association was stronger among girls than boys.

claim_text: Social media use causes depression in adolescents.
snippet: the study does not establish that social media use causes depression
label: unsupported
rationale: Source explicitly does not establish causation; the claim overclaims causality from a correlational study.

claim_text: Social media use has a large effect on adolescent depressive symptoms.
snippet: Effects were small in absolute terms
label: contradicts
rationale: Source reports effects were small in absolute terms, contradicting a large-effect claim.

claim_text: Social media use explained less than one percent of the variance in adolescent well-being.
snippet: explained well under one percent of the variation in well-being
label: supports
rationale: Source states social media explained well under one percent of variance.

claim_text: The authors endorse strong causal claims about digital technology and adolescent well-being.
snippet: caution against strong causal claims
label: contradicts
rationale: Source cautions against strong causal claims, contradicting an endorsement claim.

claim_text: Greater digital technology use is associated with slightly lower adolescent well-being.
snippet: negative but very small
label: supports
rationale: Source reports a negative but very small association, directly supporting a 'slightly lower' characterization.

claim_text: Reducing adolescents' social media use would improve their well-being.
snippet: caution against strong causal claims given the tiny effect sizes
label: unclear
rationale: Source is correlational with tiny effects and makes no claim about interventions; the effect of reducing use is unclear from this evidence.

claim_text: The evidence linking adolescent social media use to mental health harm is weak and inconsistent.
snippet: the evidence linking adolescent social media use to mental health harm is weak and inconsistent
label: supports
rationale: Directly stated by the source.

claim_text: Social media use clearly and consistently harms adolescent mental health.
snippet: weak and inconsistent
label: contradicts
rationale: Source concludes the evidence is weak and inconsistent, contradicting a clear-and-consistent harm claim.

claim_text: Reported associations between social media use and harm have clear practical significance.
snippet: of unclear practical significance
label: contradicts
rationale: Source says associations are of unclear practical significance, contradicting the claim.

claim_text: Higher social media use was associated with greater perceived social isolation in young adults.
snippet: higher social media use was associated with greater perceived social isolation
label: supports
rationale: Directly stated by the source.

claim_text: Social media use causes social isolation.
snippet: cannot determine the direction of causation
label: unsupported
rationale: Cross-sectional design cannot determine causation; the claim overclaims causality.

claim_text: The association between social media use and perceived social isolation was dose-dependent.
snippet: The relationship was dose-dependent across quartiles of use
label: supports
rationale: Source reports a dose-dependent relationship across quartiles of use.

claim_text: More time on social media predicted later internalizing problems in adolescents.
snippet: more time on social media predicted later internalizing problems
label: supports
rationale: Directly stated in this longitudinal cohort.

claim_text: This study established that social media use causes internalizing problems.
snippet: residual confounding cannot be ruled out
label: unsupported
rationale: Source notes residual confounding cannot be ruled out, so causation is not established.

claim_text: Heavy social media use may increase adolescents' risk of mental health problems.
snippet: had elevated risk of internalizing symptoms
label: weakly_supports
rationale: A longitudinal elevated-risk finding weakly supports a hedged ('may increase') risk claim given confounding caveats.

claim_text: Higher social media use was associated with depression, anxiety, and distress in adolescents.
snippet: Higher levels of social media use were associated with each of the three outcomes
label: supports
rationale: Source associates higher use with all three outcomes.

claim_text: The reviewed studies used rigorous objective measures with little methodological variation.
snippet: substantial methodological heterogeneity and heavy reliance on self-report measures
label: contradicts
rationale: Source notes substantial heterogeneity and heavy self-report reliance, contradicting a rigor/objectivity claim.

claim_text: Social media use is most harmful to younger adolescents specifically.
snippet: depression, anxiety, and psychological distress in adolescents
label: unclear
rationale: Source does not break results down by age, so an age-specific claim cannot be resolved from this evidence.
